title: Cameras, Spring Arms & a Top-Down Adventure
html:
The camera is a component, not a magic eye

In Unreal, what the player sees is just a CameraComponent attached somewhere in an Actor's hierarchy. The Player Controller picks a Pawn to possess, and if that Pawn (or its Character) has a camera marked as the active view target, that camera renders to the screen. Everything else about camera feel — distance, lag, collision, angle — comes from how you mount that camera. The workhorse for mounting is the Spring Arm (USpringArmComponent), a component that holds the camera out at a fixed length and smoothly pulls it in when something gets between the camera and the Pawn.

Spring arm anatomy

A spring arm has three properties you will touch constantly:

  • Target Arm Length — how far the camera sits from the arm's origin. For top-down, this is large (800–1200 units).
  • Camera Lag — when enabled, the camera eases toward its target position instead of snapping. Camera Lag Speed controls how snappy.
  • Do Collision Test — pulls the camera in if a wall blocks the view. For a strict top-down you usually turn this off so the camera never dives.

For a Zelda-like top-down adventure you want the camera looking almost straight down, slightly angled, with no rotation following the player. Build the Pawn hierarchy as Root → SpringArm → Camera, then rotate the spring arm down.

Tip.
The spring arm's rotation, not the camera's, decides the viewing angle. Leave the camera's relative rotation at zero and rotate the arm. That keeps your mental model simple: the arm aims, the camera just rides.

Setting up the top-down pawn in C++

Here is a Character with a fixed top-down rig. The key trick is bUsePawnControlRotation = false on the arm and orienting movement to the velocity so the character faces where it walks.

TopDownHero.cpp
// In the constructor SpringArm = CreateDefaultSubobject<USpringArmComponent>(TEXT("SpringArm")); SpringArm->SetupAttachment(RootComponent); SpringArm->TargetArmLength = 900.f; SpringArm->SetRelativeRotation(FRotator(-60.f, 0.f, 0.f)); // pitch down SpringArm->bUsePawnControlRotation = false; // arm ignores look input SpringArm->bDoCollisionTest = false; // don't dive on walls SpringArm->bEnableCameraLag = true; SpringArm->CameraLagSpeed = 8.f; TopCamera = CreateDefaultSubobject<UCameraComponent>(TEXT("TopCamera")); TopCamera->SetupAttachment(SpringArm, USpringArmComponent::SocketName); TopCamera->bUsePawnControlRotation = false; // Movement: rotate the mesh toward motion, not the controller GetCharacterMovement()->bOrientRotationToMovement = true; GetCharacterMovement()->RotationRate = FRotator(0.f, 540.f, 0.f); bUseControllerRotationYaw = false;

Moving relative to the world, not the camera

Because the camera is fixed, top-down movement should map input straight to world axes: pushing up moves north regardless of facing. Bind two Enhanced Input axes and add movement input on the world X and Y.

Move.cpp
void ATopDownHero::Move(const FInputActionValue& Value) { const FVector2D Axis = Value.Get<FVector2D>(); AddMovementInput(FVector(1.f, 0.f, 0.f), Axis.Y); // world forward AddMovementInput(FVector(0.f, 1.f, 0.f), Axis.X); // world right }

Rooms and interactables

A top-down adventure is built from rooms. The cleanest approach is one Trigger Box per room; on overlap, snap the camera's spring-arm target so each room frames itself (the classic "room-by-room" Zelda feel). For interactables — chests, levers, signs — define a simple Blueprint Interface BPI_Interact with one function Interact. Any Actor that implements it responds to the player's interact key. The player does a short sphere trace forward, and if it hits an Actor implementing the interface, calls Interact on it.

Lab.
Create BPI_Interact, implement it on a chest that plays an open animation and spawns a pickup, and have the player fire a sphere trace on the interact action. Print the hit Actor's name to confirm the trace finds it before wiring the call.
🎮 Build This: Top-Down Adventure — Hollow Glen

This starts a brand-new genre: the top-down adventure (Zelda-like). It does not extend the platformer; it is your first room-based 3D-perspective game and becomes the base for the twin-stick shooter next lesson.

  1. Make a TopDownHero Character with the Root → SpringArm → Camera rig above; pitch the arm to -60 and length 900.
  2. Set bOrientRotationToMovement = true and map Move to world X/Y so input is camera-independent.
  3. Build three rooms from BSP/static meshes connected by doorways; put a Trigger Box in each.
  4. Add a BPI_Interact interface; implement it on a chest and a lever (lever opens a door Actor).
  5. Give the hero a melee swing: an interact-style forward sphere trace that applies damage to enemies via UGameplayStatics::ApplyDamage.
  6. Add one patrolling slime enemy with simple health; killing it drops a heart pickup that restores the hero.

title: Aiming, Projectiles & a Twin-Stick Shooter
html:
Two sticks, two jobs

A twin-stick shooter splits control: the left stick moves the character, the right stick aims the weapon, and the two are independent. That decoupling is the whole genre — you can strafe left while firing right. You already have a top-down character from the last lesson; here you separate facing from movement and add a projectile that spawns from the muzzle in the aim direction.

Aiming with a stick (and with a mouse)

The right stick gives you a 2D vector. Convert it to a yaw with atan2 and set the character's control rotation so the body faces the aim. For mouse-and-keyboard players, do a line trace from the cursor to the ground plane and aim at the hit point instead. Both feed the same DesiredAimYaw.

Aim.cpp
void ATwinStickPawn::AimStick(const FInputActionValue& V) { const FVector2D A = V.Get<FVector2D>(); if (A.SizeSquared() < 0.1f) return; // deadzone const float Yaw = FMath::RadiansToDegrees(FMath::Atan2(A.X, A.Y)); SetActorRotation(FRotator(0.f, Yaw, 0.f)); } void ATwinStickPawn::AimMouse() { FHitResult Hit; GetController<APlayerController>()->GetHitResultUnderCursor( ECC_Visibility, false, Hit); if (!Hit.bBlockingHit) return; FVector Dir = Hit.ImpactPoint - GetActorLocation(); Dir.Z = 0.f; SetActorRotation(Dir.Rotation()); }
Tip.
Because the pawn now rotates by aim, turn OFF bOrientRotationToMovement from the top-down setup. Movement and facing are independent now — that is the defining difference between an adventure character and a twin-stick character.

The projectile Actor

A projectile in Unreal is an Actor with a collision shape, a mesh, and a ProjectileMovementComponent. The movement component flies it forward at InitialSpeed along the Actor's forward vector — so you just spawn it facing the aim and it travels. Bind its OnComponentHit to apply damage and destroy itself.

Bullet.cpp
ABullet::ABullet() { Collision = CreateDefaultSubobject<USphereComponent>(TEXT("Col")); Collision->InitSphereRadius(12.f); Collision->SetCollisionProfileName(TEXT("Projectile")); RootComponent = Collision; Move = CreateDefaultSubobject<UProjectileMovementComponent>(TEXT("Move")); Move->InitialSpeed = 2200.f; Move->MaxSpeed = 2200.f; Move->ProjectileGravityScale = 0.f; // top-down, no drop InitialLifeSpan = 3.f; // auto-destroy } void ABullet::BeginPlay() { Super::BeginPlay(); Collision->OnComponentHit.AddDynamic(this, &ABullet::OnHit); } void ABullet::OnHit(UPrimitiveComponent*, AActor* Other, UPrimitiveComponent*, FVector, const FHitResult& H) { if (Other && Other != GetOwner()) UGameplayStatics::ApplyPointDamage(Other, 25.f, GetActorForwardVector(), H, nullptr, this, nullptr); Destroy(); }

Firing from the muzzle

Add an empty SceneComponent named Muzzle in front of the pawn. On the fire action, spawn the bullet at the muzzle's world transform and set its Owner so the bullet skips the shooter.

Fire.cpp
void ATwinStickPawn::Fire() { const FTransform T = Muzzle->GetComponentTransform(); FActorSpawnParameters P; P.Owner = this; P.Instigator = this; GetWorld()->SpawnActor<ABullet>(BulletClass, T, P); }

Wave enemies

Enemies spawn in waves from a WaveManager Actor. Keep a count of living enemies; when it hits zero, wait a beat and spawn the next, larger wave. Each enemy uses a tiny chase: every tick, move toward the player with AddMovementInput((Player-Self).GetSafeNormal()). No NavMesh needed for an open arena.

Watch out.
Spawning every bullet from scratch will hitch under heavy fire. For a real twin-stick you will want object pooling (covered later), but for the build below, capping fire rate and using InitialLifeSpan keeps actor counts sane.
🎮 Build This: Twin-Stick Shooter — Glen Overrun

This extends the top-down adventure from p3-9: reuse its level, rig, and pickups, but swap the adventure character for a twin-stick pawn and turn the rooms into a survival arena.

  1. Duplicate the top-down hero; turn OFF bOrientRotationToMovement and add the AimStick/AimMouse rotation.
  2. Add a Muzzle scene component and a Fire action with a 0.15s cooldown.
  3. Build the ABullet Actor with ProjectileMovementComponent and point-damage on hit.
  4. Create a chaser enemy that drives toward the player each tick and has 50 HP.
  5. Add a WaveManager: spawn N enemies, detect zero alive, then spawn N+3 next wave; show the wave number on screen with a debug string.
  6. Drop the heart pickup from the adventure on every fifth kill to tie the two games together.

title: Character Movement & a 3D Third-Person Base
html:
The Character class does the hard part for you

Everything 3D in this track — the third-person base, the FPS, the TPS — stands on Unreal's ACharacter class and its UCharacterMovementComponent (CMC). The CMC is one of the most battle-tested pieces of the engine: it handles walking, falling, jumping, crouching, swimming, slope handling, step-up over small ledges, and network prediction, all out of the box. Your job is mostly to configure it and feed it input.

Pawn vs Character

A Pawn is any possessable Actor. A Character is a specialized Pawn that already contains a Capsule collider, a SkeletalMesh, and the CharacterMovementComponent. Use a bare Pawn for vehicles or spaceships where you want custom motion; use a Character for anything that walks. For humanoid 3D games, always start from Character.

ClassGives youUse for
PawnPossession, input, a root componentVehicles, drones, custom movement
CharacterCapsule + Mesh + CMC + jump/crouchWalking humanoids, most 3D games

The over-the-shoulder rig

Third-person camera is the same spring-arm pattern as top-down, but now the arm follows the controller's look rotation and sits behind and over one shoulder. Set bUsePawnControlRotation = true on the arm so mouse/right-stick look orbits the camera, and offset the camera socket to the right shoulder.

ThirdPersonBase.cpp
SpringArm = CreateDefaultSubobject<USpringArmComponent>(TEXT("SpringArm")); SpringArm->SetupAttachment(RootComponent); SpringArm->TargetArmLength = 350.f; SpringArm->SocketOffset = FVector(0.f, 60.f, 60.f); // over the shoulder SpringArm->bUsePawnControlRotation = true; // look orbits camera SpringArm->bEnableCameraLag = true; FollowCamera = CreateDefaultSubobject<UCameraComponent>(TEXT("Cam")); FollowCamera->SetupAttachment(SpringArm, USpringArmComponent::SocketName); FollowCamera->bUsePawnControlRotation = false; // Body turns toward movement; controller only drives camera bUseControllerRotationPitch = false; bUseControllerRotationYaw = false; bUseControllerRotationRoll = false; GetCharacterMovement()->bOrientRotationToMovement = true; GetCharacterMovement()->RotationRate = FRotator(0.f, 500.f, 0.f);

Movement input relative to the camera

Unlike top-down, third-person movement is camera-relative: "forward" means the direction the camera faces, flattened to the ground. Pull the controller's yaw, build forward/right vectors from it, and feed them to AddMovementInput.

Move.cpp
void AThirdPersonBase::Move(const FInputActionValue& V) { const FVector2D Axis = V.Get<FVector2D>(); const FRotator YawOnly(0.f, GetControlRotation().Yaw, 0.f); const FVector Fwd = FRotationMatrix(YawOnly).GetUnitAxis(EAxis::X); const FVector Right = FRotationMatrix(YawOnly).GetUnitAxis(EAxis::Y); AddMovementInput(Fwd, Axis.Y); AddMovementInput(Right, Axis.X); } void AThirdPersonBase::Look(const FInputActionValue& V) { const FVector2D L = V.Get<FVector2D>(); AddControllerYawInput(L.X); AddControllerPitchInput(L.Y); }

Tuning the CharacterMovementComponent

The CMC has dozens of knobs. The ones you will adjust on day one:

  • Max Walk Speed — top ground speed; default 600.
  • Jump Z Velocity — launch speed for jumps; default 420.
  • Air Control — 0–1, how much you can steer mid-air.
  • Braking Deceleration Walking — how fast you stop; lower feels icy.
  • Gravity Scale — multiplies world gravity for floatier or heavier jumps.
Tip.
For responsive controls, raise MaxAcceleration and keep GroundFriction high. "Floaty" usually means low acceleration or low braking, not low gravity — fix acceleration before touching gravity.
Rule of thumb.
Let the controller drive only the camera (yaw/pitch off on the pawn) and let the CMC's bOrientRotationToMovement turn the body. When you later add aiming (TPS), you flip this so the body follows the controller. Master the default first.
🎮 Build This: 3D Foundations — Third-Person Playground

This is a concept/foundation build, not a full genre game. It starts the 3D portion of the track and becomes the literal base class you extend in the Animation, FPS, and TPS lessons.

  1. Create a ThirdPersonBase Character with the over-the-shoulder spring-arm rig above.
  2. Set up Enhanced Input for Move, Look, and Jump; verify the body turns toward motion while the camera orbits.
  3. Tune the CMC: Max Walk Speed 600, Jump Z 500, Air Control 0.3, Braking 1500.
  4. Build a small obstacle playground: ramps, a gap to jump, low ledges to test step-up, and a high wall to confirm you cannot pass.
  5. Add a sprint action that raises Max Walk Speed to 900 while held.
  6. Drop in the Mannequin skeletal mesh (no animation yet) so you can see facing — animation comes next lesson.

title: Animation Blueprints, State Machines, Blend Spaces & Montages
html:
From a sliding mannequin to a living character

Your third-person base moves but T-poses or foot-slides because nothing drives its skeleton. An Animation Blueprint (Anim BP) is a special Blueprint that runs every frame on a SkeletalMesh, reads the character's state (speed, in-air, etc.), and outputs a final pose. It has two graphs: the Event Graph, where you compute variables, and the Anim Graph, where you blend animations into the output pose.

The four tools you will use forever

ToolWhat it doesExample
State MachineSwitches between animation states on conditionsIdle → Run → Jump → Fall
Blend SpaceBlends animations along axes (speed, direction)Idle↔Walk↔Run by speed
MontagePlays a one-shot clip with sections & notifiesAttack swing, reload
Aim OffsetAdditively leans the upper body to lookAiming up/down in TPS

Feeding the Anim BP from the character

In the Anim BP's Event Graph, cache the owning pawn once, then each update read the values the Anim Graph needs. Do this in C++ via UAnimInstance for performance, or in Blueprint for speed of iteration.

HeroAnimInstance.cpp
void UHeroAnimInstance::NativeUpdateAnimation(float Dt) { Super::NativeUpdateAnimation(Dt); ACharacter* C = Cast<ACharacter>(TryGetPawnOwner()); if (!C) return; const FVector V = C->GetVelocity(); GroundSpeed = V.Size2D(); // drives blend space bIsInAir = C->GetCharacterMovement()->IsFalling(); bIsAccel = C->GetCharacterMovement()-> GetCurrentAcceleration().SizeSquared() > 0.f; // Strafe direction for a 2D blend space (-180..180) Direction = CalculateDirection(V, C->GetActorRotation()); }
Tip.
Expose Anim BP variables with UPROPERTY(BlueprintReadOnly, Category="Anim"). They appear as nodes in the Anim Graph and can be dragged onto state-transition rules and blend-space inputs.

The locomotion state machine

Create a state machine with three states: Grounded, Jump, Fall. Inside Grounded, plug in a 1D Blend Space with GroundSpeed on the X axis blending Idle (0) → Walk (200) → Jog (450) → Sprint (700). Transitions:

  • Grounded → Fall: when bIsInAir is true.
  • Fall → Grounded: when bIsInAir is false (with a small landing blend).
  • Grounded → Jump: on a jump notify, then auto-advance to Fall.

Blend spaces for directional movement

For strafing (needed in the TPS), use a 2D Blend Space: X = Direction (-180 to 180), Y = Speed. Place the eight directional run clips at the grid corners so walking sideways plays the strafe-right clip while facing forward. The Anim BP samples it with Direction and GroundSpeed.

Montages for attacks

A Montage plays a one-shot over whatever the state machine is doing, on a dedicated slot. Add an Upper Body slot in the Anim Graph blended over the locomotion pose with a Layered blend per bone node (blending from the spine). Trigger it from gameplay:

Attack.cpp
void AHero::Attack() { if (UAnimInstance* Anim = GetMesh()->GetAnimInstance()) { if (!Anim->Montage_IsPlaying(AttackMontage)) Anim->Montage_Play(AttackMontage); } }

Inside the montage, add an Anim Notify at the moment the sword connects. The Anim BP receives AnimNotify_Hit and you run the damage trace exactly on that frame — animation and gameplay stay in sync.

Watch out.
Root motion vs in-place: if your attack clip uses root motion, enable Root Motion on the montage and set the mesh's anim mode accordingly, or the character will teleport. For looping locomotion, keep root motion OFF and let the CMC move the capsule.
🎮 Build This: Animation — Animated Third-Person Hero

This extends the 3D third-person base from p3-11. Same character; now it actually animates. The animated hero becomes the body used by both the FPS and TPS builds.

  1. Create HeroAnimInstance (C++ or BP) computing GroundSpeed, bIsInAir, bIsAccel, and Direction.
  2. Build a locomotion state machine: Grounded (1D blend space Idle→Jog→Sprint), Jump, Fall.
  3. Add a 2D strafe blend space for later TPS use and verify it with manual variable overrides.
  4. Import an attack animation, make it a Montage on an Upper Body slot, and add a hit Anim Notify.
  5. Wire the attack action to Montage_Play and run a sphere damage trace on the notify.
  6. Confirm: walking blends smoothly, jumping enters Fall, landing returns to Grounded, attacking plays over the run.

title: Building a Real 3D First-Person Shooter
html:
First-person is a camera choice plus a weapon system

An FPS is a true 3D game where the camera sits at the character's eyes and the weapon fires along the camera's aim. You will reuse the ACharacter base but mount the camera differently, add arms/weapon meshes that only the owner sees, and build a weapon that supports both hitscan (instant line trace) and projectile firing.

The first-person camera rig

No spring arm here — the camera attaches near the head with bUsePawnControlRotation = true so mouse look rotates the view directly. Add a separate first-person mesh (the arms) parented to the camera so it bobs with the view, and set it to be visible only to the owner.

FPSCharacter.cpp
FPCamera = CreateDefaultSubobject<UCameraComponent>(TEXT("FPCamera")); FPCamera->SetupAttachment(GetCapsuleComponent()); FPCamera->SetRelativeLocation(FVector(0.f, 0.f, 64.f)); // eye height FPCamera->bUsePawnControlRotation = true; ArmsMesh = CreateDefaultSubobject<USkeletalMeshComponent>(TEXT("Arms")); ArmsMesh->SetupAttachment(FPCamera); ArmsMesh->SetOnlyOwnerSee(true); // only this player sees arms ArmsMesh->bCastDynamicShadow = false; GetMesh()->SetOwnerNoSee(true); // hide the full body from self // In an FPS the body faces where you look bUseControllerRotationYaw = true; GetCharacterMovement()->bOrientRotationToMovement = false;
Tip.
SetOnlyOwnerSee on the arms and SetOwnerNoSee on the body is the classic FPS trick: you see floating arms; everyone else (in multiplayer) sees a full body. Single-player still benefits — no clipping your own torso into the camera.

Hitscan: the line trace shot

Hitscan weapons (pistols, rifles) hit instantly. Trace a line from the camera forward to a max range; whatever LineTraceSingleByChannel hits is the target. Apply damage at the impact point.

Hitscan.cpp
void AFPSCharacter::FireHitscan() { const FVector Start = FPCamera->GetComponentLocation(); const FVector End = Start + FPCamera->GetForwardVector() * 10000.f; FHitResult Hit; FCollisionQueryParams Q; Q.AddIgnoredActor(this); if (GetWorld()->LineTraceSingleByChannel( Hit, Start, End, ECC_Visibility, Q)) { DrawDebugLine(GetWorld(), Start, Hit.ImpactPoint, FColor::Red, false, 0.1f); UGameplayStatics::ApplyPointDamage( Hit.GetActor(), 20.f, FPCamera->GetForwardVector(), Hit, GetController(), this, nullptr); } }

Projectile firing reused

For a rocket/plasma weapon, reuse the ABullet pattern from the twin-stick lesson, but spawn it at a muzzle socket on the arms and orient it to FPCamera->GetForwardVector() so it flies where you aim, not where the gun barrel happens to point. A weapon EFireMode enum lets one weapon class do both.

Fire.cpp
void AFPSCharacter::Fire() { if (FireMode == EFireMode::Hitscan) { FireHitscan(); return; } const FVector Loc = ArmsMesh->GetSocketLocation(TEXT("Muzzle")); const FRotator Rot = FPCamera->GetComponentRotation(); FActorSpawnParameters P; P.Owner = this; P.Instigator = this; GetWorld()->SpawnActor<ABullet>(BulletClass, FTransform(Rot, Loc), P); }

Shootable targets and damage

Make targets that respond to ApplyPointDamage by overriding TakeDamage, subtracting health, and destroying or popping when health hits zero. A reusable UHealthComponent keeps this off your character and target classes alike.

Rule.
Drive all damage through TakeDamage/ApplyDamage rather than calling target methods directly. It is the engine's standard channel, works across hitscan and projectiles, and later replicates cleanly for multiplayer.
🎮 Build This: First-Person Shooter (3D) — Range Day

This starts a brand-new genre: the 3D first-person shooter. It reuses the ABullet projectile from the twin-stick lesson and the Character base, but the first-person camera rig and arms make it a genuinely new game.

  1. Create an FPSCharacter with the eye-height camera, owner-only arms, and owner-hidden body.
  2. Set bUseControllerRotationYaw = true so the body faces the aim.
  3. Implement hitscan firing with a debug line and point damage at 10,000 units.
  4. Add a projectile fire mode reusing ABullet, spawned from the Muzzle socket along the camera forward.
  5. Build a UHealthComponent and shootable pop-up targets that fall when destroyed.
  6. Make a shooting gallery room: static targets, a moving target on a timeline, and a score counter incremented on each kill (debug string for now).

title: Over-the-Shoulder Third-Person Shooter
html:
The shooter that watches your own back

A third-person shooter (TPS) keeps the over-the-shoulder camera from your 3D base but layers on combat-grade aiming: an aim mode that pulls the camera in tight, the body strafing to face the camera instead of the movement direction, an aim offset that leans the spine up and down, and a weapon that fires from a socket toward the camera's crosshair. You are combining the 3D base (p3-11), the animation system (p3-12), and the weapon ideas (p3-13).

Two locomotion modes

The defining mechanic: in free mode the body turns toward movement (run-and-gun fun); in aim mode the body faces the camera so the character strafes. You toggle the CMC and rotation flags on an aim action.

AimMode.cpp
void ATPSCharacter::SetAiming(bool bAim) { bIsAiming = bAim; bUseControllerRotationYaw = bAim; // body faces camera GetCharacterMovement()->bOrientRotationToMovement = !bAim; GetCharacterMovement()->MaxWalkSpeed = bAim ? 250.f : 600.f; // Pull the camera in over the shoulder when aiming TargetArmLength = bAim ? 150.f : 350.f; TargetShoulder = bAim ? FVector(0,40,55) : FVector(0,60,60); } void ATPSCharacter::Tick(float Dt) { Super::Tick(Dt); SpringArm->TargetArmLength = FMath::FInterpTo( SpringArm->TargetArmLength, TargetArmLength, Dt, 10.f); SpringArm->SocketOffset = FMath::VInterpTo( SpringArm->SocketOffset, TargetShoulder, Dt, 10.f); }
Tip.
Interpolating the arm length and socket offset (rather than snapping) is what gives the satisfying "shoulder zoom" of Gears/The Division. FInterpTo with a speed around 10 feels crisp without being instant.

Aim offset: leaning the upper body

When aiming, the character must point the weapon up or down with the camera. That is an Aim Offset — a special blend space sampled by pitch (and yaw). In the Anim BP, compute the aim pitch as the delta between the control rotation and the actor rotation, then feed it to the aim-offset node, blended over the locomotion pose on the upper-body bones.

TPSAnimInstance.cpp
void UTPSAnimInstance::NativeUpdateAnimation(float Dt) { Super::NativeUpdateAnimation(Dt); ACharacter* C = Cast<ACharacter>(TryGetPawnOwner()); if (!C) return; GroundSpeed = C->GetVelocity().Size2D(); bIsAiming = Cast<ATPSCharacter>(C)->IsAiming(); // Aim pitch for the aim-offset blend space (-90..90) const FRotator Delta = (C->GetControlRotation() - C->GetActorRotation()).GetNormalized(); AimPitch = Delta.Pitch; AimYaw = Delta.Yaw; }

Strafe blend space

Because the body no longer turns toward motion while aiming, you need the 2D strafe blend space from the animation lesson — Direction on X, Speed on Y — so moving sideways plays the side-step animation while the chest stays pointed at the target. Switch the Anim Graph between the 1D locomotion blend space (free mode) and the 2D strafe blend space (aim mode) using bIsAiming.

Firing toward the crosshair

The camera and the muzzle are not at the same place, so naive muzzle-forward shots miss where the crosshair points. Fix it with a two-trace solution: trace from the camera through the crosshair to find the target point, then fire from the muzzle toward that point.

TPSFire.cpp
void ATPSCharacter::Fire() { const FVector CamStart = FollowCamera->GetComponentLocation(); const FVector CamEnd = CamStart + FollowCamera->GetForwardVector() * 10000.f; FHitResult Look; FCollisionQueryParams Q; Q.AddIgnoredActor(this); GetWorld()->LineTraceSingleByChannel(Look, CamStart, CamEnd, ECC_Visibility, Q); const FVector Aim = Look.bBlockingHit ? Look.ImpactPoint : CamEnd; const FVector Muzzle = GetMesh()->GetSocketLocation(TEXT("Muzzle")); FHitResult Shot; if (GetWorld()->LineTraceSingleByChannel( Shot, Muzzle, Aim, ECC_Visibility, Q)) UGameplayStatics::ApplyPointDamage(Shot.GetActor(), 25.f, (Aim - Muzzle).GetSafeNormal(), Shot, GetController(), this, nullptr); }
Watch out.
Forget the two-trace solution and your shots will drift left because the camera is over the right shoulder. Always resolve the crosshair point from the camera first, then aim the muzzle at it.
🎮 Build This: Third-Person Shooter (3D) — Shoulder Squad

This extends the 3D third-person base (p3-11) and its animated hero (p3-12). Same rig and skeleton; you add aim mode, strafing, aim offset, and a TPS weapon to make a real third-person shooter.

  1. Subclass the third-person base as TPSCharacter; add the SetAiming toggle with camera zoom interpolation.
  2. In the Anim BP, switch between the 1D locomotion and 2D strafe blend spaces on bIsAiming.
  3. Add an aim-offset blend space driven by AimPitch/AimYaw over the upper body.
  4. Implement the two-trace fire (camera crosshair → muzzle) with point damage.
  5. Add cover-style waist-high blocks the player can stand behind; enemies use the chaser AI from the twin-stick lesson.
  6. Place a crosshair UMG widget and a hit-marker that flashes when ApplyPointDamage succeeds.

title: Chaos Physics & a Physics Puzzle
html:
Chaos is Unreal's physics engine

Since UE5, the built-in physics engine is Chaos. It simulates rigid bodies (boxes, balls, ragdolls), constraints (hinges, springs), and destruction (fracturing meshes into pieces). Any primitive component can become a rigid body by enabling Simulate Physics; from there the engine moves it under gravity, collisions, and any forces you apply. A physics puzzle is built almost entirely from these primitives plus the forces and constraints you wire up.

Turning an Actor into a rigid body

Enable simulation on a primitive (Static Mesh, Box, Sphere) and Chaos takes over its transform. Mass, damping, and gravity scale tune how it behaves.

PhysCrate.cpp
APhysCrate::APhysCrate() { Mesh = CreateDefaultSubobject<UStaticMeshComponent>(TEXT("Mesh")); RootComponent = Mesh; Mesh->SetSimulatePhysics(true); Mesh->SetMassOverrideInKg(NAME_None, 50.f, true); Mesh->SetLinearDamping(0.2f); Mesh->SetAngularDamping(0.5f); Mesh->SetCollisionProfileName(TEXT("PhysicsActor")); }

Forces and impulses

There are two ways to push physics: forces (applied continuously over time, like wind or a fan) and impulses (an instant kick, like an explosion or a bat hit). Use AddForce in Tick for sustained pushes and AddImpulse / AddRadialImpulse for one-shots.

Forces.cpp
// A continuous fan that pushes crates upward (per-tick) void AFanVolume::PushActor(UPrimitiveComponent* Body) { Body->AddForce(FVector(0, 0, 120000.f)); // force = mass*accel, so scale by mass-ish } // An explosion barrel: radial impulse on everything nearby void AExploBarrel::Detonate() { UGameplayStatics::ApplyRadialDamage(this, 100.f, GetActorLocation(), 400.f, nullptr, {}, this); for (TActorIterator<AActor> It(GetWorld()); It; ++It) if (auto* P = It->FindComponentByClass<UPrimitiveComponent>()) if (P->IsSimulatingPhysics()) P->AddRadialImpulse(GetActorLocation(), 400.f, 1500.f, ERadialImpulseFalloff::RIF_Linear, true); Destroy(); }
Tip.
AddForce is mass-dependent (heavier objects move less for the same force); AddImpulse with bVelChange=true ignores mass and changes velocity directly. Use the velocity-change form when you want the same launch regardless of object weight.

Constraints: hinges, springs, and joints

A Physics Constraint component links two bodies and limits how they can move relative to each other. Lock all axes and free one rotation for a hinge (a door, a see-saw); add linear limits with stiffness for a spring (a bouncy platform). This is how you build levers, swinging bridges, and balance puzzles.

Hinge.cpp
Constraint = CreateDefaultSubobject<UPhysicsConstraintComponent>(TEXT("C")); Constraint->SetupAttachment(RootComponent); Constraint->SetConstrainedComponents(FrameMesh, NAME_None, DoorMesh, NAME_None); Constraint->SetAngularSwing1Limit(EAngularConstraintMotion::ACM_Free, 0); Constraint->SetAngularSwing2Limit(EAngularConstraintMotion::ACM_Locked, 0); Constraint->SetAngularTwistLimit (EAngularConstraintMotion::ACM_Locked, 0);

Destruction with Geometry Collection

Chaos can fracture a mesh into pieces using the Fracture mode, producing a Geometry Collection. The pieces stay glued (connected) until enough force or damage breaks the cluster apart. Set a damage threshold and feed it impulses (from your explosion barrel) to shatter walls on cue — great for a puzzle gate that you blow open.

Rule.
Physics objects need a collision profile that blocks the things they should rest on and overlaps triggers. A crate that sits on the floor but activates a pressure plate must block WorldStatic and overlap the plate's trigger — set this on the profile, not per-frame.
🎮 Build This: Physics Puzzle — Pressure

This starts a brand-new genre: the physics puzzle / push-the-box room. It does not extend a prior game; it is a fresh Chaos-driven sandbox (and the obstacle-course ideas carry into the racer's hazards next lesson).

  1. Make a PhysCrate rigid body the player can push by walking into it (CMC pushes physics if bEnablePhysicsInteraction is on).
  2. Build a pressure plate: a trigger that opens a door Actor when a crate (or the player) rests on it.
  3. Add a hinge-constrained see-saw the player loads with crates to balance and reach a ledge.
  4. Place a fan volume that lifts a lightweight crate upward via per-tick AddForce.
  5. Add an explosion barrel that radial-impulses crates and fractures a Geometry Collection wall blocking the exit.
  6. Gate the level exit behind solving all three mechanisms in sequence; print "Solved!" on completion.

title: Vehicles, Chaos Wheels & a 3D Racing Game
html:
Cars are a specialized physics Pawn

Unreal drives vehicles with the Chaos Vehicle plugin. A car is a Pawn whose root is a UChaosWheeledVehicleMovementComponent attached to a physics-simulating skeletal mesh. The movement component reads four (or more) wheel setups, applies engine torque, steering, and braking, and runs a suspension simulation per wheel. You configure the engine, transmission, and wheels; the engine handles the rest of the rigid-body driving.

Tip.
Enable the ChaosVehiclesPlugin in Edit → Plugins and restart, and add ChaosVehicles to your module's PublicDependencyModuleNames in the .Build.cs before any of this code will compile.

The vehicle pawn

Start from AWheeledVehiclePawn. Its mesh must be a physics-asset skeletal mesh with wheel bones. Grab the movement component to set engine and steering values.

RaceCar.cpp
ARaceCar::ARaceCar() { auto* Move = Cast<UChaosWheeledVehicleMovementComponent>( GetVehicleMovementComponent()); Move->EngineSetup.MaxTorque = 700.f; Move->EngineSetup.MaxRPM = 6000.f; Move->TransmissionSetup.bUseAutomaticGears = true; Move->DifferentialSetup.DifferentialType = EVehicleDifferential::RearWheelDrive; // Chase camera on a spring arm behind the car SpringArm = CreateDefaultSubobject<USpringArmComponent>(TEXT("Arm")); SpringArm->SetupAttachment(GetMesh()); SpringArm->TargetArmLength = 650.f; SpringArm->SocketOffset = FVector(0, 0, 200.f); SpringArm->bEnableCameraLag = true; SpringArm->CameraLagSpeed = 5.f; SpringArm->bInheritPitch = false; // stay level over bumps ChaseCam = CreateDefaultSubobject<UCameraComponent>(TEXT("Cam")); ChaseCam->SetupAttachment(SpringArm, USpringArmComponent::SocketName); }

Wheels: the heart of the feel

Each wheel is a UChaosVehicleWheel subclass (a data asset) defining radius, width, friction, suspension travel, and whether it steers or is driven. Front wheels steer; rear wheels drive (for RWD). Suspension SpringRate and DampingRatio control bounce and grip.

Wheel propertyEffect
FrictionForceMultiplierGrip — higher sticks, lower drifts
MaxSteerAngleHow sharply front wheels turn
SuspensionMaxRaise/DropTravel range over bumps
bAffectedByEngineWhether the wheel receives torque

Driving input

Feed throttle, steering, brake, and handbrake straight into the movement component. These are normalized -1..1 (steer/throttle) or 0..1 (brake/handbrake).

Drive.cpp
void ARaceCar::Throttle(const FInputActionValue& V) { GetVehicleMovement()->SetThrottleInput(V.Get<float>()); } void ARaceCar::Steer(const FInputActionValue& V) { GetVehicleMovement()->SetSteeringInput(V.Get<float>()); } void ARaceCar::Brake(const FInputActionValue& V) { GetVehicleMovement()->SetBrakeInput(V.Get<float>()); } void ARaceCar::Handbrake(const FInputActionValue& V) { GetVehicleMovement()->SetHandbrakeInput(V.Get<bool>()); }
Watch out.
If the car sinks through the floor or wheels float, the physics asset's wheel bones do not match the wheel setup names, or the collision is set to QueryOnly. Vehicles need simulation-enabled physics and a chassis collision body that blocks WorldStatic.

The track and the timer

Build a track from road meshes (or splines), then add checkpoint trigger volumes in order. A RaceManager records the start time when the player crosses the start line, validates each checkpoint is hit in sequence (so you cannot cut the course), and on crossing the finish reports the lap time. Store the best time for a time-trial.

Rule.
Always validate checkpoints in order. Without ordered checkpoints, players drive a tiny loop across start/finish and post impossible times. The manager should ignore a finish trigger until every numbered checkpoint has been passed.
🎮 Build This: Racing (3D) — Time Trial Circuit

This starts a brand-new genre: the 3D racing / car game. It is a fresh build on the Chaos Vehicle system, though it reuses the spring-arm chase-camera pattern from the 3D character lessons and can borrow the physics hazards from the puzzle room.

  1. Enable the ChaosVehicles plugin; create an ARaceCar from AWheeledVehiclePawn with a chassis mesh and physics asset.
  2. Configure front/rear wheel data assets: front steers, rear driven, tuned friction and suspension.
  3. Set engine torque/RPM, automatic transmission, and RWD differential; add the chase camera on a spring arm.
  4. Map throttle, steering, brake, and handbrake via Enhanced Input.
  5. Lay out a closed-loop track with ordered checkpoint triggers and start/finish line.
  6. Build a RaceManager that times the lap, enforces checkpoint order, shows current/best time on screen, and adds a physics-hazard chicane (knock-over cones reused from the puzzle crates).
